content:
Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry Wancen Liu a,b, Ruohan Xia a, Guoxue Li a, Wenhai Luo a,*, Long D. Nghiem b,** a Beijing Key Laboratory of Farmland Soil Pollution Prevention and Remediation, College of Resources and Environmental Sciences, China Agricultural University, Beijing, 100193, Chinab Centre for Technology in Water and Wastewater, University of Technology Sydney, Ultimo, NSW, 2007, Australia

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Biogas slurryUltrafiltration concentration3D fouling structureFoulant interaction

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: A B S T R A C T This study investigated the formation and evolution of membrane fouling during the ultrafiltration (UF) con­centration of chicken manure biogas slurry. The fouling layer was systematically investigated using spectroscopic and compositional analyses, along with three-dimensional (3D) structure characterization to elucidate under­lying fouling mechanisms. Results revealed that membrane foulants adhered to the surface in a non-uniform fashion, leading initially to an anisotropic cake layer and rapid water flux decline exceeding 40% as water re­covery reached 10%. UF membrane demonstrated effective rejection of humic acid and divalent cations (e.g., Ca and Mg) for nutrient enrichment, which in turn led to complex organic-inorganic fouling. Among organic fou­lants, high molecular weight humic acid showed the strongest membrane affinity, followed by protein, low molecular weight humic acid and polysaccharide-like substances. These organic foulants subsequently facilitated the co-deposition of inorganic salts, such as KCl, K2SO4 and KPO3, on membrane surface. As water recovery increased, Ca2+ and Mg2+ enhanced the cross-linking of organics foulants, resulting in the evolution of fouling layer structure from anisotropic to isotropic. Notably, the crystallization of potassium salts and enhanced interaction between K+ and dissolved organic matter exacerbated pore blockage.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: isotropic. Notably, the crystallization of potassium salts and enhanced interaction between K+ and dissolved organic matter exacerbated pore blockage. These findings underscore the critical roles of humic substances and potassium in membrane fouling to shed lights on their targeted control to improve the efficiency and lifespan of UF-based biogas slurry treatment systems.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: 1. Introduction Biogas projects have been extensively implemented for managing urban and rural organic waste streams, including sewage sludge, food waste, and livestock manure [1,2]. Through anaerobic biological treat­ment, these organic wastes are converted to methane-rich biogas for renewable energy recovery [3,4]. The by-product of this process, known as digestate or biogas slurry, is rich in valuable nutrients, including ammonium, phosphate, amino acids, and humus, making it a high-quality liquid fertilizer for farmland application [5,6]. However, a persistent challenge remains. The continuous generation of biogas slurry often exceeds the seasonal demand for fertilization, underscoring the need for efficient treatment and volume reduction strategies. Membrane filtration technology has been widely employed as a se­lective barrier that efficiently removes suspended solids and microor­ganisms while retaining beneficial minerals, thereby producing high- quality effluent for discharge [7]. In particular, ultrafiltration (UF) has gained significant attention for biogas slurry treatment, especially for achieving the dual objectives of water extraction and nutrient recovery, such as multivalent ions and humus [8]. However, the broader appli­cation of UF is greatly limited by membrane fouling [9]. For example, during the concentration of chicken manure biogas slurry, the UF water flux decreased by 32 % within only 50 min of operation [10].

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: [9]. For example, during the concentration of chicken manure biogas slurry, the UF water flux decreased by 32 % within only 50 min of operation [10]. This rapid fouling is primarily attributed to the high content of sus­pended solid, dissolved organic matter (DOM), and inorganic salts in biogas slurry. These components contribute to fouling mainly through pore blocking and cake layer formation [11]. Although valuable nutri­ents are successfully concentrated in the retentate, they also become the predominant contributors to membrane fouling, thereby restricting the broader adoption of UF for biogas slurry concentration [12]. Conse­quently, recent research efforts have increasingly focused on elucidating the mechanisms of fouling formation and evolution for developing

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: Available online 18 August 2025 0376-7388/Crown Copyright 2025 Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). effective control strategies. While membrane foulants can be determined from a series of mature characterization such as Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction analysis, their dynamic evolution and sequential deposition within the fouling layer remain poorly understood. Recent advances in spectroscopic methods have enabled more detailed in­vestigations. For instance, two-dimensional correlation spectroscopy (2D-COS) has been deployed to track the sequential deposition of organic foulants on the membrane surface [13,14]. In addition, time-of-flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) has been utilized to reveal the three-dimensional (3D) distribution of foulants within nanoscale fouling layer, owing to its high depth resolution (~5 nm) and simultaneous detection of both inorganic and organic species [15]. Most existing studies have focused on the use of synthetic wastewater to elucidate the formation of fouling layer after long-term membrane operation [16]. For instance, it has been identified that calcium car­bonate predominated within the cake layer, while aluminium and sili­con oxides were observed only at the surface during UF operation for drinking water treatment using ToF-SIMS analysis [17].

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: , while aluminium and sili­con oxides were observed only at the surface during UF operation for drinking water treatment using ToF-SIMS analysis [17]. These findings highlight the influence of foulant composition on the spatial structure of fouling layer. In addition, distinct combinations of organic foulants lead to various cake structures, where more isotropic layers tend to mitigate membrane fouling [18]. Given the inherently complex composition of biogas slurry after anaerobic digestion of wastewater and/or organic wastes, there is an urgent need to further track to the spatiotemporal dynamics of fouling layers during its treatment by membrane-based processes to offer trade-off strategies for resource recovery and fouling mitigation. This study aimed to investigate the dynamic evolution of membrane fouling during UF concentration of biogas slurry for resource enrich­ment. Water flux and classical blocking filtration models were revealed to signal membrane fouling behaviors for periodic foulant autopsy. The compositions of organic and inorganic foulants in the fouling layer were quantified. Furthermore, the individual and interactive contributions of organic foulants, mainly including humic acid, protein, and poly­saccharide, to fouling formation were evaluated. Furthermore, mem­brane fouling structure and its dynamic evolution was tracked by examining the surface and cross-sectional morphology, 2D-COS anal­ysis, and ToF-SIMS 3D reconstruction.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: cture and its dynamic evolution was tracked by examining the surface and cross-sectional morphology, 2D-COS anal­ysis, and ToF-SIMS 3D reconstruction. Findings from this study provide valuable insights into fouling dynamics to facilitate future membrane modification for foulant detachment to sustain resource recovery from high strengthen waste streams. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Biogas slurry Biogas slurry was collected from an anaerobic digestion plant for chicken manure disposal in JiNan (China). Chicken manure was selected given its high nutrient content. The plant was equipped with an indus­trial continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) and a digestate liquid-solid separator. Biogas slurry for this study was the liquid fraction from digestate liquid-solid separator. The plant has been stably operated for several years at the mesophilic condition of 35 ± 1 ◦C. Biogas slurry was processed for sedimentation, screen filtration using 200 and 50 mesh, and then centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 15 min to reduce the content of suspended solids. Key physicochemical properties of biogas slurry are presented in Supplementary Information (SI) Table S1. 2.2. Experimental system and protocol A lab-scale cross-flow UF system was used in this study. The UF system consisted of a 1 L stainless feed reservoir, a high-pressure pump (BT600-longer pump, Bona, China), a stainless flat sheet membrane cell, and a 1 L plastic permeate reservoir (SI Fig. S1).

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: rvoir, a high-pressure pump (BT600-longer pump, Bona, China), a stainless flat sheet membrane cell, and a 1 L plastic permeate reservoir (SI Fig. S1). The membrane cell was fitted with a flat sheet polyether sulfone (PES) membrane coupon (PES100, Bona, China) with the pore size of 100 KDa and an effective area of 56 cm2. The membrane coupon was mounted horizontally to avoid foulant deposition by gravity. In addition, the system was operated in cross-flow mode to mitigate compaction and minimize fouling accumulation. The UF system was operated at a constant pressure of 0.6 MPa and a cross-flow velocity of 0.42 m s-1. Such high pressure was applied to secure the desirable initial and then stable water flux for the filtration of biogas slurry with complex characteristics. It has been reported that biogas slurry contains high contents of organic substances, suspended solids and colloids to induce high viscosity and thus hinder water permeation through the membrane [19]. The temperature of biogas slurry in feed reservoir was maintained at 25 ± 1 ◦C using a temperature control unit (DFY-10L/10, Yuhua Instrument, China). A digital scale (PR6201 ZH/E, Ohaus, USA) was used to monitor the weight of permeate reservoir to automatically record flux variation. All UF filtration tests were repeated for three times using a new membrane coupon. Prior to biogas slurry concentration, deionized water (1 L) was used to stabilize the UF membrane to obtain a constant initial flux.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: membrane coupon. Prior to biogas slurry concentration, deionized water (1 L) was used to stabilize the UF membrane to obtain a constant initial flux. UF filtration tests were terminated at specific water recovery from 2 to 60 % to track membrane fouling dynamics. The fouled membrane coupons were taken from the cell for subsequent autopsy. 2.3. Analysis methods 2.3.1. Physicochemical characteristics of biogas slurry Biogas slurry, concentrate, and permeate were sampled for analyses. The potassium dichromate oxidation method was used to determine humic acid. Total phosphorus (TP) was measured by the ammonium molybdate spectrophotometric method. All liquid samples were filtered through 0.45 μm aquo-system Millipore filters prior to final analysis of organic substances and inorganic salts in the aqueous phase. The total organic carbon (TOC) and total nitrogen (TN) was measured by a TOC/ TN analyzer (TOC-VCSH, Shimadzu, Kyoto). Key cations, including Na, K, Ca, and Mg, were analyzed using an ion chromatography (Aquion, ThermoFisher, USA). 2.3.2. Fouling morphology and characterization Pristine and fouled membranes were dried in a desiccator for sub­sequent surface and cross-section characterization. Membrane crosssection samples were obtained by liquid nitrogen freezing and then snapping [20].

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: ub­sequent surface and cross-section characterization. Membrane crosssection samples were obtained by liquid nitrogen freezing and then snapping [20]. A scanning electron microscope (MIRA LMS, TESCAN, Czech Republic) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (Essence, TESCAN, Czech Republic) (SEM-EDS) was used for identi­fying membrane morphology and element compositions of fouled membrane (both surface and cross section) after platinum plating. An atomic force microscope (AFM) (Dimension Icon, Bruker, Germany) was used to measure the roughness of membrane surface. A contact angle apparatus (LSA100, Lauda Scientific, Czech Republic) was employed to determine static contact angles of membrane and foulant samples. An attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectrum FTIR spectrometer (ATR-FTIR, Nicolet iS20, Thermo fisher scientific, USA) was used to identify functional groups of organic foulants on membrane surface. The ATR-FTIR results were further imported for 2D-COS anal­ysis to track organic fouling under different water recoveries. The auto-peaks of synchronous map and cross-peaks of every two bands of asynchronous map within the wavenumber of 600–4000 cm-1 were identified. Crystals on fouled membrane were determined using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) apparatus (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) at the diffraction angle of 5◦–90◦.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: Crystals on fouled membrane were determined using an X-ray diffraction (XRD) apparatus (Ultima IV, Rigaku, Japan) at the diffraction angle of 5◦–90◦. The diffraction patterns were analyzed using JADE software for phase identification based on the International Centre for Diffraction Data (ICDD) database. 2.3.3. ToF-SIMS analysis The chemical components of fouling layer were determined by ToF-

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content:
W. Liu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124571

SIMS ION-TOF instrument (GmbH, Münster, Germany; model M6) based on the mass-to-charge ratio of secondary ions. Depth profiles were generated by plotting the intensities of representative secondary ions (correlated with foulant concentration) against sputtering time (corre­lated with depth), thus revealing the distribution of chemical compo­nents along the fouling layer depth. The corresponding 3D structures of key foulants, including protein, inorganic ions, and salt precipitates, were reconstructed from the depth profile data using SurfaceLab 7 (IONTOF GmbH). Two representative secondary ions, C3H8N+ (m/z = 58) and C4H8N+ (m/z = 70), were selected for the identification of protein [21]. Furthermore, ion fragments such as CxHy, CxHyO, and CxHyN(O) were used to represent general organic foulants, as specific identifica­tion of humic acids and polysaccharides was limited due to the complexity of real biogas slurry. 2.3.4. Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix analysis Organic foulants on the membrane surface were extracted using the method reported previously [22,23]. Specifically, a 14 cm2 of fouled membrane coupon was cut and then soaked in 50 mL of NaOH (0.01 M) for rigorous mixture at 160 rpm for 24 h to extract organic foulants for further measurement. The 0.01 M NaOH was used as it provides a mildly alkaline condition for effective organic dissolution and low oxidation.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: This concentration is much lower than the concentrations of typical cleaning solutions (e.g., 0.1 M) known to cause degradation of organic matter [8]. The phenol-sulfuric acid method and modified Lowry method were employed to quantify polysaccharide, protein and humic acid, respectively. A 3-dimensional excitation-emission matrix (3D-EEM) fluorescence spectrometer (F96Pro, Lingguang, Japan) and then parallel factor analysis (PARAFAC) was applied to determine fluorescent components in extracted solution [24]. Although individual polysaccharides are non-fluorescent, their hybrids with proteins and/or humic acids exhibit fluorescence and then EEM signals. Thus, the in­dependent and synergistic interactions among the three organic foulants were determined using multiple linear regression combined with vari­ance partitioning analysis [13,25]. Specifically, the fluorescent intensity at each excitation-emission position and the concentrations of organic foulants were processed for wavelength-wise multiple linear regression analysis. Variance partitioning analysis was utilized to evaluate both individual and interactive contribution of the X terms to total R-squared (R2) of the multiple linear regression model. Key ions in extracted so­lution were quantified using an ion chromatography (Aquion, Thermo­Fisher, USA). 2.3.5. Filtration modelling Membrane fouling behavior was further deciphered by four mem­brane blocking models proposed by Hermia [26,27].

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: sher, USA). 2.3.5. Filtration modelling Membrane fouling behavior was further deciphered by four mem­brane blocking models proposed by Hermia [26,27]. Fouled UF mem­branes after biogas slurry concentration were processed for physical cleaning to determine reversible fouling propensity. The thickness of the fouling layer was determined from cross-sectional SEM images using ImageJ (v 1.8) by averaging measurements taken at five randomly selected positions on each image. Water recovery was calculated as follows: Water recovery = VV0 * 100%Eq. (1) where V0 was the initial volume of biogas slurry, and V was the volume of collected permeate. The rejection rate was determined to evaluate membrane separation performance as: Rejection rate =(1 -Cp C0 )* 100%Eq. (2) where C0 was the concentration in feed biogas slurry, and Cp was the concentration in permeate. Based on Fick’s Law [28], the diffusion coefficient (D) of certain foulant increased with the increase of η (concentration polarization exists). Adsorption potential (η) was determined to evaluate the pref­erential adsorption of different foulants on the membrane, and was calculated as: ηi = mtotal,i madsorbed,i = Cs,iVi C0,iAEq.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: ned to evaluate the pref­erential adsorption of different foulants on the membrane, and was calculated as: ηi = mtotal,i madsorbed,i = Cs,iVi C0,iAEq. (3) where mtotal,i was the total amount of foulants in the concentrate (mg) at the water recovery of i%; madsorbed was the total amount of foulants attached on membrane surface (mg) at the water recovery of i%; A represents the total surface area of membrane (cm2), and Vi was the solution volume of concentrate (L) at the water recovery of i%; Cs,i and C0,i represent the content of foulants in concentrate (mg/L) and per unit membrane surface area (mg/cm2) at the water recovery of i%, respectively.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Biogas slurry characteristics and fouling behavior during UF concentration The UF membrane demonstrated effective rejection of humic-like organics and multivalent cations, while the rejection of potassium (a monovalent cation) was significantly lower (Fig. 1A–D). Humic acid rejection ranged from 80 % to 95 % with an increase in response to membrane fouling. Such high rejection was possibly due to the strong aggregation tendency of humic acids [29], which could be further enhanced by complexation with diand trivalent cations in biogas slurry [30]. Indeed, the molecular weights of organic substances in biogas slurry are lower than the nominal cut-off of the used UF membrane (SI Fig. S2A), but their aggregation forms larger assemblies with particle sizes to exceed 0.5 μm (SI Fig. S2B), allowing effective rejection. Multivalent cations such as Ca2+ and Mg2+, though not inherently retained by UF membranes, can form complexes with humic acids and are thus rejected together [31]. In contrast, monovalent cations like K+, which do not readily form such complexes, were less effectively rejected [32]. As a result, the rejection of K+ by the UF membrane was sub­stantially lower than that of Ca2+ and Mg2+ (Fig. 1C and D). The UF membrane also exhibited moderate rejection of TP while that of TN was insignificant (Fig. 1E and F).

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: han that of Ca2+ and Mg2+ (Fig. 1C and D). The UF membrane also exhibited moderate rejection of TP while that of TN was insignificant (Fig. 1E and F). In this study, TN and TP were mainly composed of ammonium and phosphate, respectively, comprising about 80 % of their totals (SI Table S1). The observed moderate TP rejection was likely due to phosphate precipitation [12]. On the other hand, the insignificant TN rejection was consistent with the ineffectively rejection of ammonia nitrogen. Humic acid enrichment in the feed solution was observed as water recovery increased. On the other hand, no discernible change in K+ concentration was observed with increasing water recovery, due to its low rejection by the membrane. In contrast, the concentrations of Ca2+ and Mg2+ in the concentrate initially increased (0–40 % water recov­ery), likely as a result of their association with humic acid retained in the concentrate. However, their levels subsequently declined at higher water recovery (45–60 %). This trend suggests that the rejection of divalent cations was mainly governed by their interactions with a fouling layer rich in humic acid-like organic compounds. The concen­tration of humic acid was an order of magnitude higher than these metal ions (Fig. 1A–D). In addition to humic acid – cation complexation, the rejection of Mg2+ and especially Ca2+ could be also contributed by their sulfide, sulfate, or phosphate precipitation [33].

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: – cation complexation, the rejection of Mg2+ and especially Ca2+ could be also contributed by their sulfide, sulfate, or phosphate precipitation [33]. A rapid decline, followed by gradually decrease and then a relative stabilization, in water flux was observed during UF concentration of biogas slurry (Fig. 1G). It is noteworthy that the initial flux of UF membrane was only 20 L m2 h-1, which is lower than that reported for wastewater treatment (e.g. municipal wastewater) [34]. Such low initial water flux cold be attributed to the inherent characteristics of biogas slurry, such as high organic content, salinity, and viscosity (SI Table S1). At the beginning of UF operation (first 20 min), the sharp drop in flux corresponded to the onset of foulant deposition and cake layer formation on the membrane surface [35]. The subsequent gradual flux decline could be attributed to the compaction of cake layer. Although the water flux remained stable at the water recovery of 40–60 %, intensified in­teractions among foulants aggravated irreversible membrane fouling, as evidenced by a significant increase in fouling resistance and a reduction in flux recovery after physical cleaning (SI Fig. S3A&B). The entire filtration process lasted approximately 13 h (765 min), during which a continuous flux monitoring was conducted to capture fouling dynamics.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: he entire filtration process lasted approximately 13 h (765 min), during which a continuous flux monitoring was conducted to capture fouling dynamics. Further modelling results evidenced the cake formation at the initial water recovery of up to 10 %, followed by cake layer extension and secondary pore constriction due to foulant–foulant interactions (SI Fig. S3C). To further elucidate the causes of observed flux variations, membrane fouling at a specific water recovery was systematically tracked.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content:
W. Liu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124571

3.2. Spectroscopic analysis and quantification of the fouling layer 3.2.1. Membrane morphology and hydrophobicity characterization Morphology measurement of pristine and fouled UF membranes revealed significant foulant aggregation on the membrane surface (Fig. 2). SEM images from cross-sectional and surface perspectives showed that foulants rapidly formed cake layer (Fig. 2A&B), with a thickness of about 5 μm at the initial water recovery of just 2 % (Fig. 2C). This initial layer provided adhesion sites for further foulant accumulation and consolidation, ultimately impairing filtration effi­ciency (Fig. 1G). Foulant deposition, characterised by scattered bumps also significantly altered the membrane roughness (Ra) (Fig. 2D). Within the water recovery range of 0–30 %, Ra fluctuated between 4.3 and 15.3 nm, with the highest of 23.0 nm at the water recovery of 5 %, and then 20.0 nm at 60 %. The rough surface, likely caused by irregular inorganic scaling, resulted in a highly hydrophilic membrane surface (contact angle of 46◦) (SI Fig. S4A).

Fig. 2. (A) Cross-section of membrane and fouling layer, (B) membrane surface morphology, (C) cross-section thickness of fouled membranes, and (D) surface roughness of membrane at different water recoveries (data are shown as the mean ± standard deviation of three replicating experiments). W. Liu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124571

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: 3.2.2. Spectroscopic analysis Spectroscopic analysis of the fouling layer evidenced the develop­ment of anisotropic fouling cake layer as the water recovery increased. EDS analysis revealed the presence of elements including C, O, N, P, S, Ca, Mg and K on the membrane surface (Fig. 3A). XRD further evidenced direct precipitation of inorganic foulants on membrane surface, with the presence of KCl, KPO3, Mg3(PO4)2 and CaSO4 (Fig. 3B), when comparing to the standard Powder Diffraction File (Fig. 3C). EDS and XRD showed insignificant alteration in the composition of inorganic crystals. Notably, increased O content of fouled membrane at the water recovery of 60 % corresponded to higher salts precipitation, such as sulfate, and phos­phate, correlating with a rise in surface roughness (Fig. 2C). FTIR spectra exhibited characteristic peaks of alkoxy C–O–C at 1030 cm-1, aliphatic alcohol C–O at 1150 cm-1, aromitic group at 1536 cm-1, aromatic C––O and C––C moieties at 1575 cm-1, N–H (amide II) group at

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: 1650 cm-1 and 3295 cm-1 (Fig. 3D), indicating the presence of polysaccharide-like [18], low molecular weight (LMW) humic acid-like [36], high molecular weight (HMW) humic acid-like [37], and protein-like substances, respectively. In addition, peaks at 1713 cm-1 and 723 cm-1 corresponded to C–H and C–C vibration in PES mem­brane, with weakened intensities to suggest membrane fouling. FTIR spectra showed significant variation as water recovery increased to 10 %. Such variation diminished as water recovery increased further to 30 %, beyond which, no discernible changes in FTIR spectra were observed (Fig. 3D). The variation of FTIR spectra during the early stages of UF operation could be attributed to the transition from an anisotropic fouling cake structure at low water recovery to a more isotropic structure as water recovery exceeded 30 %. 2D-COS analysis confirmed the anisotropic structure as water re­covery increased to 10 %, with the sequential deposition of organic

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: Fig. 3. (A) Elemental composition of surface, (B) XRD spectra, (C) Standard powder diffraction file (PDF) cards, and (D) FTIR spectra of fouled membrane at different water recoveries. The PDF reference patterns were obtained using the JADE software, which accesses the ICDD database for phase identification. W. Liu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124571 foulants (Table 1). Specifically, the variation in spectral intensity at the band of 1150 cm-1 occurred before that of 1575 cm-1, followed by the change at 1713 cm-1 and 723 cm-1. Subsequently, simultaneous change occurred at 3295 cm-1 and 1650 cm-1, followed by 1536 cm-1, and finally at 1030 cm-1. Although FTIR cannot directly determine molec­ular weight, variations in peak intensity and position can indicate compositional changes and can be further correlated with differences in molecular size or structure. In addition, PARAFAC analysis revealed that humic substances include HMW humic-like substances (C1 and C2) and LMW fulvic-like substances (C3) (SI Fig. S5). As such, of the four organic foulants, HMW humic acid-like substances were assumed to be firstly attached, followed by protein-like, LMW humic acid-like, and finally polysaccharide-like substances. The preferential attachment of HMW humic acid-like substances was attributed to their fast diffusion rate and the presence of aliphatic alcohol hydroxyl groups [18,37], which enhanced their adhesion to pristine PES membrane.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: ed to their fast diffusion rate and the presence of aliphatic alcohol hydroxyl groups [18,37], which enhanced their adhesion to pristine PES membrane. Protein-like substances served as a connector between HMW and LMW humic acid-like compounds, likely due to their embedding effect and hydrophobic interactions [38]. By contrast, polysaccharide-like substances were less likely to deposit on the mem­brane surface, due to their large, curved molecular structure and weak hydrogen bonding capability [18]. The synchronous map showed no prominent auto-peaks beyond 30 % water recovery (SI Fig. S4D), indicating the formation of a uniform, isotropic fouling layer. This transition from anisotropic to isotropic structure was possibly driven by the involvement of multivalent cations, which facilitated the bridging of organic foulants. Wu et al. [39] observed that a sandwich-like cake layer of humic acids and poly­saccharides became isotropic in the presence of Ca2+. In the present study, a notable decline in Ca2+ and Mg2+ contents in the concentrate were observed at water recoveries of exceeding 30 % (Fig. 1C and D), suggesting their active participation in the formation of the fouling structure. The isotropic fouling layers could alleviate membrane fouling to indicate that pre-treatment strategies that facilitate their rapid for­mation could potentially sustain flux performance [18]. 3.2.3.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: brane fouling to indicate that pre-treatment strategies that facilitate their rapid for­mation could potentially sustain flux performance [18]. 3.2.3. Quantification of organic and inorganic foulants composition Inorganic constituents dominated the fouling layer as water recovery increased to 10 % (Fig. 4). Beyond 10 % water recovery, additional fouling was dominated by organic foulants, correlating with reduced surface roughness (Fig. 2C). Along with the foulant enrichment in the concentrate, all organic foulants increased variably (Fig. 4A). In general, DOM is mainly composed of humic substances, proteins, and poly­saccharides (SI Table S1), and is the main component of the fouling layer, thereby inducing flux reduction during wastewater treatment. Of the three organic foulants, more protein and humic acid were observed as water recovery increase to 10 %, evidencing their stronger affinity onto pristine membrane (Table 1). Humic acid content rose signifi­cantly, exceeding the combined levels of protein and polysaccharide as water recovery increased to 30 %, to exhibit a humic acid-dominated fouling layer. The presence of polar functional groups (e.g., carboxyl and hydroxyl) imparts amphiphilic properties, increasing membrane surface hydrophilicity (SI Fig. S4A). K, Na, Ca and Mg were main inorganic contributors (Fig. 4B). While K content increased steadily, Na showed little change with increasing water recovery.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: Na, Ca and Mg were main inorganic contributors (Fig. 4B). While K content increased steadily, Na showed little change with increasing water recovery. Cross-sectional EDS mapping revealed deeper K pene­tration into the membrane matrix, whereas Na remained on the surface (SI Fig. S6). XRD analysis further confirmed the absence of Na crystal­lization (Fig. 3C), indicating its limited role in fouling due to its inability to participate in foulant interactions [39]. The increase in Ca and Mg within the fouling layer at the end of operation further supported their interaction with humic acid-like organics.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: 3.3. Interactions among foulants in membrane fouling formation 3.3.1. Roles of interactions between organic foulants To elucidate the contributions of organic foulants to membrane fouling, multiple linear regression - variance partitioning analysis (MLRVPA) was applied (Fig. 5). Results showed that at the initially low water recovery, organic fouling was largely contributed by humic acid, fol­lowed by protein and then polysaccharide alone (Fig. 5A). As recovery increased (15–60 %), humic acid and protein continued to act primarily in isolation, with minor synergistic effects observed between humic acid and polysaccharide (Fig. 5B). Polysaccharide alone and its ternary interaction with other organic foulants contributed minimally. These findings suggested that organic foulants mainly acted independently rather than synergistically, which supports their spatial separation in the fouling layer, contributing to the formation of an anisotropic structure, as further confirmed by the 2D-COS analysis. Such independent accu­mulation could be further promoted by cross-linking with multivalent cations (Fig. 4B). 3.3.2. Role of cation in membrane fouling formation ToF-SIMS analysis showed similar types of foulants as water recovery increased to 60 %, with only slight variations in their intensities (Table 2). A high proportion and diverse morphology of K-related fragments were observed, which is consistent with its elevated concen­tration in the fouling layer (Fig. 4B).

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: K–DOM complexes (1.0–3.9 %) and inorganic precipitates (3.1–5.6 %), mainly as KCl, K2SO4 and KPO3. In contrast, apart from minor NaCl, no stable Na precipitates with anions such as SO42-, PO43-were detected, indicating Na mainly existed in an adsorbed state. In addition, Na ions cannot participate in organic foulant interactions, and the aggregation behavior between different organic foulants was barely influenced by Na+ [39], which is consistent with the relatively unchanged Na+ contents in the fouling layer (Fig. 4B). Fouling layer was mainly composed of organic foulants, K+ and Na+, uniformly distributed with lower intensities near the membrane inter­face due to the initial layer formation (Fig. 6A). The distribution of inorganic crystals was visualized, showing increased salt accumulation over filtration time as ion adsorption reached saturation. Consequently, intensities of K2SO4, KPO3 and KCl rose with an increase in water re­covery, leading to the aggravated blockage of fouling layer (Fig. 1F). Besides, a higher intensity of K-DOM was observed, indicating a stronger ionic bonding between K and organic foulants. Hot spots of K2SO4 and KCl became more pronounced on the fouling layer surface at the water recovery of 5 % and 60 % (Fig. 6A). This result is consistent with the elevated Ra values (Fig. 2C). A significant increase in divalent cations, in particularly Mg, was observed at a high water recovery (Fig. 6B), evidencing its role to enhance multi-organic foulants [40].

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: in divalent cations, in particularly Mg, was observed at a high water recovery (Fig. 6B), evidencing its role to enhance multi-organic foulants [40]. Overall, K salts, K-DOM complexes, and Ca/Mg bridging jointly contributed to intensified fouling layer blockage and denser fouling layer development during membrane fouling evolution. 3.3.3. Sequential deposition of foulants on the membrane surface Composition of the cake fouling layer was uniform when examined in 2D over the membrane surface (Fig. 6A). However, the vertical dimension showed clear stratification. For instance, C and K+ showed a deeper sputter depth than Ca2+ and Mg2+. Such discrepancy indicated the asynchronous attachment of foulants on membrane surface to form an anisotropic structure. A longer sputtering time means a deeper sputtering depth [41]. As showed in Fig. 6B, at the water recovery of 2 %, the normalized intensities of both C, S+, and cations remained stable during the initial sputtering (approximately 0–480 s). Cations sharply decreased thereafter, while S+ increased significantly around 550 s, suggesting that the sputtering had reached the interface between the fouling layer and the PES membrane. Afterwards, all signals declined, indicating the sputtering reached the nonwoven support layer. The C exhibited more delayed shift time of sharp reduction of intensity than cations to evidence the rapid attachment of organic foulants, particu­larly HMW humic-like acid (Fig. 3D), onto the membrane surface.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: f intensity than cations to evidence the rapid attachment of organic foulants, particu­larly HMW humic-like acid (Fig. 3D), onto the membrane surface. In contrast, protein exhibited shallower sputtering depths in the vertical direction compared to total organic foulants (SI Fig. S7). This observation was consistent with their subsequent deposition after HMW humic-like acid captured by 2D-COS measurements. Strong penetration of protein into initial fouling layer was visualized at the high water re­covery beyond 30 % (SI Fig. S7). Similar to proteins, Ca exhibited a uniform distribution and deep penetration, while Mg was predomi­nantly localized in the upper region. These results support the role of divalent ion–DOM interactions to drive a transition toward isotropic fouling structure in UF operation. Humic acid-like substances exhibited the highest adsorption poten­tial (η) compared to inorganic ions at the water recovery in the range of 0–25 % (Fig. 6C). Subsequently, similar η occurred of humic acid and divalent ions at high water recovery beyond 30 %. This observation further confirmed humic acid preferentially attached onto the pristine membrane (Fig. 4) given their high diffusivity and then induce organicdominated fouling layer (Fig. 5A). Among the ions, Ca and Mg exhibited similar η profile, with a marked increase at the water recovery of 30 %, highlighting their crucial role in forming an isotropic fouling layer (Fig. 3D).

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: ilar η profile, with a marked increase at the water recovery of 30 %, highlighting their crucial role in forming an isotropic fouling layer (Fig. 3D). The η of K experienced a notable increase at the water recoveries of 0–10 % and 50–60 % (Fig. 6C), aligning with the increased K2SO4 and KCl deposition that facilitated to hole plugging (Figs. 1F and 6A). In contrast, Na+ showed a relatively low and stable η. Na+ distribution in

content: Fig. 6. ToF-SIMS 3D reconstructed images of fouling layers at different water recoveries (A), the depth profile of fouling layers at the water recovery of 2 % (B), and adsorption potential (η) at different water recoveries. The raster size of fouled membrane was 100 μm * 100 μm. W. Liu et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124571 the upper and middle fouling layers (Fig. 6A) was consistent with EDS mapping results (SI Fig. S6), suggesting that Na deposition mainly resulted from physical adsorption rather than interaction or precipitation. 3.4. Underlying mechanisms of fouling formation during biogas slurry concentration A rapid water flux decline (approximately 40 %) occurred as the water recovery increased to 10 % due to the entrapment of membrane foulants and then rapid cake layer formation (Fig. 1G). HWM humic acid-like performed the highest favourability to attach on the membrane surface during UF treatment of biogas slurry, followed by protein, LWM humic acid-like and polysaccharide substances to form an anisotropic fouling layer at initially low water recovery. These organic foulants induced the deposition of inorganic salts, K2SO4 and KPO3. Subse­quently, divalent cation plays decisive roles in UF membrane fouling to induce an isotropic organic-dominated fouling layer at high water re­covery beyond 30 % (Fig. 7). The enhanced bridging effect between humic acid and divalent cation due to their high interception (Fig.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: at high water re­covery beyond 30 % (Fig. 7). The enhanced bridging effect between humic acid and divalent cation due to their high interception (Fig. 1), further facilitating the cross-linking and coalance of membrane foulants (Fig. 5) to form an isotropic and denser fouling layer. Meanwhile, the higher crystallization of K salts (K2SO4 and KCl) aggravated fouling layer blockage. As such, the targeted control of humic acid and/or K deposi­tion, for example, by membrane modification for detachment could be an efficient membrane-fouling-control strategy for improving the per­formance of UF-based biogas slurry treatment. Additionally, pretreatment approaches that induce the rapid formation of a more isotropic fouling layer could further contribute to flux improvement. 4. Conclusion Results from this study demonstrate that organic and inorganic foulants adhered to the membrane surface in a sequential and timedependent manner, rapidly forming an anisotropic fouling layer that significantly reduce water flux as water recovery reached 10% during UF concentration of biogas slurry. Among organic foulants, high mo­lecular weight humic acid exhibited the strongest membrane affinitive, followed by proteins, low molecular weight humic acid and then polysaccharides. These organics facilitated the co-deposition of inor­ganic salts, exacerbating membrane fouling.

citation.title: Three-dimensional analysis of foulant distribution in ultrafiltration membrane treating biogas slurry

content: molecular weight humic acid and then polysaccharides. These organics facilitated the co-deposition of inor­ganic salts, exacerbating membrane fouling. Multiple linear regres­sion–variance partitioning analysis model further evaluated the individual contributions of these organic foulants to membrane fouling. ToF-SIMS analysis revealed vertical heterogeneity of the fouling layer. In particular, the Ca2+/Mg2+ ions were primarily distributed at the surface, inducing cross-linking and forming an isotropic fouling layer at the top. As water recovery progressed, crystallization of potassium ions (K+) and dissolved organic matter intensify, leading to the aggravated blockage within the fouling layer. These findings underscore the pivotal role of humic acid and potassium to induce membrane fouling, sug­gesting that their preferential removal during pre-treatment could significantly enhance the performance and lifespan of UF-based biogas slurry treatment systems.
